content.assessment: From an international comparison lens, Hungary's current tracking arrangement is already an international outlier in intensity and timing, and the comparative evidence base strongly associates this outlier status with elevated inequality of opportunity and extreme between-school stratification. A monitoring-only status quo does not engage with any comparator reform mechanism, so there is no transferability question to assess for the mechanism itself; the relevant comparative finding is diagnostic (documenting how anomalous Hungary's configuration is) rather than prescriptive. No country studied simply monitored an equivalent selective structure as a policy response, so there is no comparable precedent to evaluate for effectiveness.
opportunities: Continued benchmarking against OECD data could eventually build the evidentiary and political case for reform if trends worsen visibly relative to peers.
strengths: Uses existing PISA/ABC infrastructure that already enables the same international benchmarking used to identify Hungary's outlier status, so continued data comparability with OECD peers is preserved.
threats: Comparative evidence suggests inequality effects of early tracking are highly consistent across studies, implying that absent mechanism change, Hungary's relative position versus OECD norms is unlikely to improve on its own.
weaknesses: No comparator country has treated monitoring alone as a sufficient response to a tracking-driven inequality profile as extreme as Hungary's, so there is no positive precedent supporting this path's adequacy.
The proposal leaves untouched the exact structural features (early selection age, high selection capacity share) that comparative data most strongly implicate in inequality outcomes.
